text: A three-centimeter transverse incision is made one centimeter below the xiphoid process. The surgeon performs blind dissection with a finger at the back of the sternum, followed by the insertion of a single-port surgery port through the subxiphoid incision.

CO₂ insufflation at eight millimeter Hg is performed, displacing the lungs and pericardium to create space behind the sternum. The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to detach the thymus from the back of the sternum. The mediastinum is dissected to open the chest cavity bilaterally.
The surgeon dissects the thymus, thymic tumor, and surrounding fatty tissue from the pericardium, carefully navigating anterior to the bilateral phrenic nerves.
The surgeon isolates the brachiocephalic vein and separates the right upper pole of the sinus. The thymic vein is cut using a vessel sealing device.
The surgeon places the resected thymus into a retrieval bag within the mediastinum and removes it through the sub-xiphoid incision.
